front: Work: when tortured about who killed the tyrannical Commander, all villagers insist that the title town 'did it'
back: Fuenteovejuna / The Sheep Well (Lope de Vega)

front: Work: a peasant steals the Commander's crossbow to prevent him from raping Laurencia
back: Fuenteovejuna / The Sheep Well (Lope de Vega)

front: Work: set during Ferdinand and Isabella's reign; they pardon the entire village at the end
back: Fuenteovejuna / The Sheep Well (Lope de Vega)

front: Work: the Commander Gomez de Guzman is from the Order of Calatrava
back: Fuenteovejuna / The Sheep Well (Lope de Vega)

front: Work: Laurencia delivers a rousing monologue calling men 'spinning women' and telling them to wear 'headdresses and petticoats'
back: Fuenteovejuna / The Sheep Well (Lope de Vega)

front: Work: the Commander orders the arrest of Esteban and hanging of Frondoso to have his way with Laurencia
back: Fuenteovejuna / The Sheep Well (Lope de Vega)

front: Work: Mengo explains that poetry is like a baker making doughnuts — some poems turn out fluffy, others get burnt
back: Fuenteovejuna / The Sheep Well (Lope de Vega)

front: Work: Laurencia says her ideal life would consist of eating a lot of food instead of being annoyed by men
back: Fuenteovejuna / The Sheep Well (Lope de Vega)

front: Work: Countess Diana falls in love with her secretary Teodoro but refuses to let him marry her maid Marcela
back: The Dog in the Manger (Lope de Vega)

front: Work: title alludes to an Aesop fable about a dog that prevents others from using what it cannot use itself
back: The Dog in the Manger (Lope de Vega)

front: Work: the gracioso Tristan fabricates a Byzantine noble lineage for a secretary to resolve the point of honor
back: The Dog in the Manger (Lope de Vega)

front: Work: Diana asks a suitor if he isn't satisfied with the flesh he already has, referring to copious gifts of meat
back: The Dog in the Manger (Lope de Vega)

front: Work: a duke ties his wife Casandra under a white veil and tricks his bastard son into stabbing her after discovering their affair
back: Punishment Without Revenge (Lope de Vega)

front: Work: a servant snuffs out a candle with the toss of a hat to cover up his master's affair
back: Punishment Without Revenge (Lope de Vega)

front: Author: nicknamed 'The Phoenix of Wits' by Cervantes, who also called him a 'Monster of Nature'
back: Lope de Vega

front: Author: believed all tragedies should only be three acts long; his comedias rejected Aristotelian unities
back: Lope de Vega

front: Author: called his lover Elena Osorio 'Filis' in his poems
back: Lope de Vega

front: Author: a poem by this author inspired the response 'Letter to Belardo' by an anonymous female poet using the alias Amarilis
back: Lope de Vega

front: Author: Goethe wished he had produced as 'vast and colourful' an oeuvre as this playwright
back: Lope de Vega

front: Author: Lorca referenced the 'duende' in this author's religious sonnets
back: Lope de Vega

front: Author: this playwright first created the character Pedro Crespo, later used by Calderon in The Mayor of Zalamea
back: Lope de Vega

front: Author: 'Sonnet Impromptu' by this author is frequently anthologized in collections of Spanish Golden Age poetry
back: Lope de Vega

front: Author: some scholars theorize this playwright wrote the fake Don Quixote sequel
back: Lope de Vega
